## What the Fields Hide

There is no signboard that adequately prepares you. You turn off the main road at a junction marked only by a chai stall, follow a narrow track through rice fields, and then — in a clearing, surrounded by nothing remarkable — you find it.

The Chausath Yogini Temple at Hirapur is circular. It is roofless. It is barely nine metres in diameter. And it contains sixty-four yogini figures carved into niches along its inner wall that are unlike anything else in Indian religious art.

## The Yoginis Themselves

Each of the sixty-four is a form of the divine feminine — powerful, autonomous, iconographically distinct. They carry different weapons, wear different expressions, stand on different vahanas (animal vehicles). Some are serene. Several are terrifying. A few are explicitly erotic. All are carved with a roughness, an immediacy, that differs sharply from the refined polish of mainstream temple sculpture.

Yogini worship belongs to the Tantric tradition — a strand of Indian spirituality that has always occupied the boundary between the sanctioned and the transgressive. The yoginis are practitioners, not passive figures. They hold power. Their iconography reflects this.

The presiding goddess stands in the centre of the inner courtyard: Maheshvari, eight-armed, above a buffalo. The quality of light that enters this roofless space falls differently depending on the time of day, illuminating different figures as the sun moves.

## The Archaeology

The temple dates to approximately the 9th century CE. It was rediscovered by scholars only in 1953. There is a second, less-visited Chausath Yogini temple at Ranipur-Jharial in western Odisha; the two are the only circular yogini temples that survive in India.

Why circular? The form may reflect a ritual practice in which initiates circumambulated the figures during ceremony. Or it may be cosmological — the circle as mandala, the sixty-four as a numerological completeness. The texts are silent on this. The temple simply stands.

## The Silence Here

I arrived on a weekday afternoon. There was one other visitor, a young woman from Kolkata doing her PhD on Tantric iconography. The caretaker sat under a tree outside, reading a newspaper.

Inside the circle, the only sound was wind through the open roof. The yoginis looked outward from their niches, toward the rice fields, toward the distant highway, toward whatever they have been looking at for eleven hundred years.

I sat on the floor in the centre of the circle for a long time, not meditating exactly, just being still in a place that asked for stillness.

The woman from Kolkata came and sat nearby. We didn't speak. We didn't need to.

---

*Location: Hirapur village, ~9 km from Bhubaneswar. ASI protected. Open daily sunrise to sunset.*
